text: Laparoscopic Repair of Perforated Gastric Ulcer with Primary Closure and Omental Patch by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a forty-eight year old female with history of chronic back pain on multiple pain medications including aspirin. Presented with sudden onset of severe abdominal pain and tenderness. Her CT scan showed free intra-abdominal air and fluid consistent with perforated viscous. She underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy and found to have perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer with significant peritonitis.

The laparoscope enters the abdomen, revealing a large perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer. There is visible contamination of gastric material into the abdominal cavity, contributing to significant peritonitis and the presence of intra-abdominal fluid.

The surgeon performs primary closure of the perforated pre-pyloric gastric ulcer using multiple intra-optic sutures with an intracorporeal suturing technique. The closure is reinforced with a second layer of sutures.

The surgeon drains the intra-abdominal fluid and irrigates the abdomen with an antibiotic solution. The omental attachments to the transverse colon are mobilized to create an omental patch. The integrity of the closure is confirmed by inflating the stomach with diluted blue dye via a nasogastric tube, showing no dye extravasation. The area is then reinforced and patched with the greater omentum.

This patient was kept MPO with IV antibiotics and nasogastric tube for three days. Then an upper GI study was performed on day number four which confirmed the passage of the contrast through the duodenum with no extravasation or obstruction. Patient was sent home with oral antibiotics and proton pump inhibitor.
